topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box sat on my doorstep like a grotesque accusation. It was addressed to 'Apartment 3B,' a unit I hadn't occupied in nearly a year, and the return address was a name I vaguely recognized from old building records. My jaw tightened until my molars ached. I snatched the package, the cheap paper rough beneath my fingers, and stormed back inside. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, ready to snap. I ripped the tape with unnecessary violence, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. Inside, nestled amongst packing peanuts, was a collection of tacky ceramic figurines—items I was certain belonged to the person who had vacated months ago. A hot flush crept up my neck, and I slammed the box onto the nearest surface, the resulting thud rattling the glassware on the shelf. "Who the hell sends this?" I muttered, my voice dangerously low, every syllable laced with pure, unadulterated fury.

[story 2]

I stared at the delivery slip, the handwriting unfamiliar and annoyingly neat. It was clearly meant for the previous occupant. A slow, burning heat began to build in my chest, spreading outward until my breath hitched. I picked up the package—a bulky, oddly shaped wooden crate—and felt a tremor run through my hands. It wasn't just anger; it was a deep, vibrating resentment. I marched to the door, my steps heavy and deliberate, each footfall an assertion of my displeasure. When I tore the package open, the contents spilled out: old, faded photographs and some sentimental junk. I didn't bother to look closely; the sight was enough. I threw the contents onto the floor, the fragile items scattering like casualties in a sudden, violent storm. "This is a joke," I hissed, kicking a stray piece of wrapping paper across the linoleum. My knuckles were white where I gripped the box, the tension in my shoulders making me feel physically ill.

[story 3]

The sight of the package stopped me dead in my tracks. It was clearly misdelivered, bearing the faded inscription of a former resident. A pressure built behind my eyes, a searing heat that made the air feel thick and suffocating. I didn't move for a long moment, just stood there, breathing in short, sharp gasps. When I finally moved, it was with a jerky, uncontrolled motion. I snatched the delivery, the weight of it feeling disproportionate to its contents. My gaze swept over the box, and a low growl escaped my throat, a sound raw and vibrating with suppressed frustration. I practically threw the package against the wall, the impact making the cheap wood groan. "Unbelievable," I spat out, my voice tight and strained. I paced the small room three times, my movements sharp and agitated, unable to settle until the feeling inside me subsided into a dull, throbbing ache.
